items.q: Which preposition expresses purpose ('in order to')?
items.why: Students treat por and para as identical 'for'. PARA = destination, purpose, recipient, deadline. POR = reason/cause, exchange, duration, motion through. The distinction is regularly tested.

items.q: What is the 'nosotros' form of 'hablar' (to speak)?
items.why: Students confuse nosotros (we) with ellos (they). AR verbs in present: hablo, hablas, habla, HABLAMOS, habláis, hablan. The nosotros ending is -amos, not -an.
Another classic faux ami. Spanish 'constipado' refers to nasal congestion (a cold). The medical 'constipated' is 'estreñido'. Confusion can lead to awkward medical conversations.

items.q: Which Spanish daily is centre-left and based in Madrid?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: El Pais. “El Mundo” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What does 'el cuarto poder' refer to?
items.why: It's The press. “The army” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which article of the 1978 Constitution protects press freedom?
items.why: Exact answer: 20. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
